questions.q: During a network partition, the CAP theorem says a distributed system must give up one of which two properties?
questions.explain: Partitions are a fact of real networks, so P is forced on you. That leaves a choice between Consistency (linearizable reads) and Availability (every request gets a non-error response).

questions.q: A datastore that keeps serving reads and writes on both sides of a partition and reconciles afterward is making which choice?
choices: CP — consistent, the minority side refuses requests
AP — available, replicas diverge and reconcile later
CA — it has opted out of partition tolerance
ACID — it is enforcing serializable transactions
questions.explain: Staying available on both sides means replicas can diverge, so it's an AP system (e.g. Cassandra, DynamoDB) that reconciles with last-write-wins, vector clocks, or CRDTs once the partition heals.

questions.q: Why is calling single-node PostgreSQL a "CA" system misleading?
choices: PostgreSQL is actually an AP system by default
CA is impossible for any database to achieve
PostgreSQL gives up consistency to gain availability
CAP's trade-off only appears during partitions, which can't happen on one node — so the label says nothing useful
questions.explain: CAP is a *during-partition* trade-off. A single machine can't partition, so 'CA' is vacuous; the label only becomes meaningful (and a real C-vs-A choice) once you replicate across nodes.
